name: Kenneth R. Koedinger
role: Co-originator of learning engineering as a named field; Cognitive Tutor pioneer; DataShop founder
description: Co-originator of learning engineering as a field. Creator of Cognitive Tutor and Carnegie Learning, deployed in thousands of schools. Founded DataShop (now LearnSphere), the world's largest open repository of educational log data. Coined the modern formulation of learning engineering as an applied, data-driven discipline. Past chair of the EDM community and co-director of LearnLab at CMU.
key_contributions: Led development of Cognitive Tutors deployed in thousands of schools; co-founded Carnegie Learning Inc.
Founded Pittsburgh Science of Learning Center (PSLC) and DataShop — world's largest open repository of educational log data
Developed the Assistment methodology linking formative assessment to intelligent hints
Coined the term 'learning engineering' in its modern formulation as an applied, data-driven discipline
Directed LearnLab, CMU's in-vivo experimentation platform for LE research
key_works: Abstract planning and perceptual chunks (with Anderson, Cognitive Science, 1990)
Intelligent tutoring goes to school in the big city (IJAIED, 1997)
Learning Engineering for Online Education (contributor, 2019)
notes: Must-include anchor person. Seed multiple topics.

name: Ryan S. J. d. Baker
affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Graduate School of Education
role: EDM pioneer; director of Penn Center for Learning Analytics; high-leverage LE policy voice
description: Pioneer in educational data mining and learning analytics. Directs the Penn Center for Learning Analytics. Co-author of High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering (2021), the field's most-cited contemporary policy document. Editor of the Journal of Educational Data Mining. Known for work on detecting gaming-the-system and off-task behavior in ITS.
key_contributions: Codified Educational Data Mining as a formal discipline with Baker & Yacef 2009 (1,500+ citations)
Developed methods for detecting gaming-the-system, off-task behavior, and boredom in ITS telemetry
Directed production of 'High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering' (2021), the field's most cited contemporary policy document
Directs the Penn Center for Learning Analytics, a major hub for LE research at scale
Editor of the Journal of Educational Data Mining
key_works: The State of Educational Data Mining in 2009 (with Yacef, JEDM, 2009)
High-Leverage Opportunities for Learning Engineering (with Boser & Shelley, 2021)
Stupid Tutoring Systems, Intelligent Humans (IJAIED, 2016)
notes: Key policy hub figure. Seed T04 and T17.

name: Bror Saxberg
affiliation: Formerly Chan Zuckerberg Initiative / Kaplan; founder of LearningForge
role: Learning science practitioner; LE thought leader; industry-academic bridge
description: Early champion of learning engineering as a named field. Wrote influential 2016 Atlantic piece calling for a new 'learning engineer' profession. Led learning science at Chan Zuckerberg Initiative, framing LE as 'precision education' analogous to precision medicine. Previously Chief Learning Officer at Kaplan. ICICLE advisory board member and co-editor of Learning Engineering for Online Education (Routledge, 2019).
key_contributions: Led learning science at Chan Zuckerberg Initiative, framing LE as 'precision education' analogous to precision medicine
As Chief Learning Officer at Kaplan, pioneered application of cognitive science to large-scale commercial education
ICICLE advisory board member; co-editor of Learning Engineering for Online Education (Routledge, 2019)
Defined the practitioner learning engineer as someone who applies learning science at 'massive, affordable scale'
Trained at MIT (BSc), Oxford (DPhil), and Harvard Medical School; bridges science and practice unusually well
key_works: Learning Engineering for Online Education (co-editor, Routledge, 2019)
Why Do So Many Students Hate School? (Atlantic, 2014)
A Transformation to Learning Engineering (Simon Initiative, 2016)
notes: Practitioner anchor. Key for T00 field-definition topics.

name: Jim Goodell
role: LE standards architect; Learning Engineering Toolkit co-editor; ICICLE community builder
description: Co-founder of the Learning Engineering book (IEEE Press). Co-editor of the canonical Learning Engineering Toolkit (Taylor & Francis, 2023). Co-chaired IEEE ICICLE, building the professional infrastructure for learning engineering. Framed LE as a verb — a process — rather than a noun. Drove development of xAPI standards and competency frameworks for interoperable LE systems.
key_contributions: Co-edited the Learning Engineering Toolkit (Taylor & Francis, 2023), the field's primary practitioner handbook
Co-chaired IEEE ICICLE, building the professional infrastructure for learning engineering
Developed the Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model with ICICLE colleagues
Framed LE as a verb (a process) rather than a noun (a role or technology)
Drove development of xAPI standards and competency frameworks for interoperable LE systems
key_works: Learning Engineering Toolkit (co-editor, Taylor & Francis, 2023)
Generalizable Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model (with Blake-Plock et al., 2024/2025)
notes: Core ICICLE community figure. Anchor for T16 (standards/professional).

name: John Behrens
role: Psychometrician; evidence-centered design architect; learning analytics at scale
description: Evidence-centered design (ECD) researcher and practitioner. Led large-scale assessment innovation at ETS and Pearson. Co-developed the ECD framework with Robert Mislevy, bridging psychometrics and instructional design. Active ICICLE contributor who defined the role of the learning engineer in assessment and credentialing contexts. Applies Bayesian inference networks to adaptive assessment design.
key_contributions: Co-developed Evidence-Centered Design (ECD) with Robert Mislevy — the principled framework for aligning assessment tasks, evidence, and competency claims
Applied ECD and learning analytics to large-scale credentialing and certification programs at ETS and Pearson
Integrated Bayesian inference networks into adaptive assessment design
Active IEEE ICICLE contributor; defined the learning engineer role within assessment and psychometric contexts
Bridged academic psychometrics and LE practice across K-12, higher education, and professional credentialing
key_works: Mislevy, Almond & Behrens — Evidence-Centered Design for Knowledge Representation in Assessment (2002)
Behrens et al. — The SR/VE Framework (in Educational Measurement, 4th ed., 2011)
DiCerbo & Behrens — Implications of the Digital Ocean on Current and Future Assessment (2012)
notes: Assessment/psychometrics anchor for LEBOK. Key for T04 and T15 (evidence standards).

name: John Liu
affiliation: MIT, Mechanical Engineering / MIT LEAP Group
role: Engineering education researcher; MIT LEAP Group PI; data-driven curriculum researcher
description: Principal investigator of the MIT Learning Engineering and Practice (LEAP) group, applying data science and LE methods to improve engineering curricula at scale. Develops methods for identifying prerequisite gaps and mapping concept dependencies from student performance data in large MIT engineering courses.
key_contributions: Directs the MIT LEAP group, applying data science and LE methods to engineering curriculum improvement at scale
Developed methods for identifying prerequisite gaps and concept dependency structures from student performance data
Applied LE approaches to large MIT engineering courses with thousands of students
Bridges MIT's engineering culture with learning sciences and the IEEE ICICLE community
Contributes data-driven curriculum redesign frameworks applicable across STEM higher education

MIT LEAP Group technical reports on concept prerequisites and curriculum analytics
notes: MIT LEAP Group PI. Engineering education LE practitioner.

name: Norman Bier
affiliation: Carnegie Mellon University — Open Learning Initiative
role: OLI Director; evidence-based online course design practitioner; open educational resources leader
description: Director of CMU's Open Learning Initiative (OLI), which produces open online courses shown to produce learning outcomes comparable to semester-length courses in half the time. Pioneered continuous data-driven redesign of online courses. Bridges the Simon Initiative's research infrastructure with open educational access goals. Active ICICLE contributor.
key_contributions: Directs CMU's Open Learning Initiative, demonstrating that evidence-based online courses can match traditional semester outcomes in half the time
Pioneered integration of formative assessments, learning analytics dashboards, and iterative course redesign in OLI
Demonstrated that continuous data-driven redesign improves course outcomes year over year
Bridged Simon Initiative research infrastructure with open educational access and equity goals
Active IEEE ICICLE contributor defining OLI's learning engineering methodology for the broader community
key_works: Lovett, Meyer & Thille — The Open Learning Initiative: Measuring the Effectiveness of the OLI Statistics Course (Educause Quarterly, 2008)
Thille et al. — Educating the Educators: Initiatives for Technology-Enhanced Learning (Schmidt & Cohen, 2014)
CMU Simon Initiative OLI technical reports on learning analytics and course redesign
notes: OLI Director. Strong connection to Simon Initiative. Evidence-based online learning.

name: Scotty D. Craig
role: Human factors researcher; virtual humans and pedagogical agents specialist
description: Professor and leader in ASU Learning Engineering Institute initiatives. Systematically investigates design principles for virtual humans and pedagogical agents in learning environments. Demonstrated that vocal quality, aesthetic design, and agent persona significantly affect learner cognitive load and outcomes. Co-authored the Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model. Bridges human factors engineering with instructional technology research.
key_contributions: Systematically investigated design principles for virtual humans and pedagogical agents in learning environments
Demonstrated that vocal quality, aesthetic design, and agent persona significantly affect learner cognitive load and outcomes
Co-authored the Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model with ICICLE colleagues
Bridges human factors engineering with instructional technology research at ASU
Active contributor to LE practitioner community through ICICLE and graduate training
key_works: Design principles for virtual humans in educational technology environments (with Schroeder, 2018)
The voice quality of pedagogical agent impacts learning and agent perceptions (with Siegle, JCAL, 2024)
Generalizable Learning Engineering Adoption Maturity Model (with Blake-Plock et al., 2024/2025)
notes: ASU LEI. Pedagogical agents / virtual humans. LEAMM co-author.

name: Herbert A. Simon
role: Originator of the 'learning engineer' concept; Nobel laureate
description: Nobel laureate in Economics (1978) who coined the term 'learning engineer' in his 1967 Educational Record essay 'The Job of a College President'. Argued that teaching is a domain of expertise separate from subject-matter knowledge, requiring its own scientific grounding. Co-developed ACT cognitive theory with John Anderson, providing the scientific basis for cognitive tutors. His broader work on bounded rationality, problem solving, and cognitive simulation laid the intellectual foundation for ITS.
key_contributions: Coined the term 'learning engineer' in the 1967 Educational Record essay 'The Job of a College President'
Argued that teaching is a domain of expertise separate from subject-matter knowledge, requiring its own scientific grounding
Championed evidence-based instruction over discovery learning when discovery imposes excessive cognitive cost
Co-developed ACT cognitive theory with John Anderson, providing the scientific basis for cognitive tutors
His broader work on bounded rationality, problem solving, and cognitive simulation laid the intellectual foundation for ITS
key_works: The Job of a College President (Educational Record, 1967)
Human Problem Solving (with Newell, 1972)
The Sciences of the Artificial (1969)
notes: Field originator. Coined 'learning engineer'. Anchor for T00 and founding era.

name: John R. Anderson
role: Cognitive architect; creator of ACT-R; pioneer of Cognitive Tutors
description: Cognitive scientist and architect of ACT-R (Adaptive Control of Thought–Rational), the dominant cognitive architecture for modeling skill learning. Creator of the LISP Tutor (1983), the first empirically validated intelligent tutoring system. Co-developed the Cognitive Tutor for algebra, deployed in 2,600+ schools to half a million students. Demonstrated that ITS-based tutoring produces learning gains equivalent to 1-to-1 human tutoring.
key_contributions: Developed ACT-R (Adaptive Control of Thought–Rational), the dominant cognitive architecture for modeling skill learning
Created the LISP Tutor (1983), the first empirically validated intelligent tutoring system
Co-developed Cognitive Tutor for algebra, deployed in 2,600+ schools to half a million students
Established the production-rule encoding methodology that allows cognitive models to drive adaptive tutoring
Demonstrated that ITS-based tutoring produces learning gains equivalent to 1-to-1 human tutoring (the '2-sigma effect')
key_works: Rules of the Mind (1993)
Cognitive Tutors: Lessons Learned (Journal of the Learning Sciences, 1995)
Abstract planning and perceptual chunks (with Koedinger, Cognitive Science, 1990)
notes: ACT-R and Cognitive Tutor. Foundational ITS figure.

name: Allen Newell
role: Co-creator of SOAR cognitive architecture; pioneer of AI and cognitive science
description: Pioneer of artificial intelligence and cognitive science. Co-developed SOAR — a unified theory of cognition — and with Herbert Simon created Logic Theorist (1956) and General Problem Solver (1957), the first AI programs. Established the 'physical symbol system hypothesis', the theoretical bridge between computation and cognition. SOAR's chunking mechanism provided an explicit computational theory of procedural skill acquisition.
key_contributions: Co-developed SOAR, a unified theory of cognition modeling problem solving and learning via chunking
With Herbert Simon, created Logic Theorist (1956) and General Problem Solver (1957), the first AI programs
Established the 'physical symbol system hypothesis' — the theoretical bridge between computation and cognition
SOAR's chunking mechanism provided an explicit computational theory of procedural skill acquisition
key_works: Human Problem Solving (with Simon, 1972)
SOAR: An Architecture for General Intelligence (with Laird & Rosenbloom, AI 1987)
Unified Theories of Cognition (1990)
notes: SOAR and cognitive simulation. Co-founder of AI.

name: John Sweller
role: Developer of Cognitive Load Theory
description: Educational psychologist who formalized Cognitive Load Theory (CLT), distinguishing intrinsic, extraneous, and germane cognitive load. Established that working memory constraints must drive instructional design decisions. CLT-derived principles (worked examples effect, split-attention effect, redundancy effect) are now standard LE design rules. Provided the scientific justification for explicit, structured instruction over unguided discovery.
key_contributions: Formalized Cognitive Load Theory (CLT) distinguishing intrinsic, extraneous, and germane load
Established that working memory constraints must drive instructional design decisions
CLT-derived principles (worked examples effect, split-attention effect, redundancy effect) are now standard LE design rules
Provided the scientific justification for explicit, structured instruction over unguided discovery
key_works: Cognitive load during problem solving: Effects on learning (Cognitive Science, 1988)
Cognitive Load Theory, Learning Difficulty, and Instructional Design (Learning and Instruction, 1994)
Cognitive Load Theory (with Ayres & Kalyuga, Springer 2011)
notes: CLT originator. Core T01 and T12 anchor.

name: Ann L. Brown
role: Pioneer of design-based research and collaborative learning environments
description: Educational psychologist and pioneer of design-based research (DBR) and collaborative learning. Her 'Design Experiments' (JLS, 1992) is the most-cited paper in the learning sciences. Co-created 'Fostering Communities of Learners', an influential model for scaffolded inquiry-based learning environments. Demonstrated how engineered classroom environments can simultaneously serve as research sites.
key_contributions: Established design-based research (DBR) as the canonical methodology for testing learning interventions in authentic contexts
Design Experiments' (JLS, 1992) is the most-cited paper in the field of learning sciences (2,800+ citations)
Co-created 'Fostering Communities of Learners' — an influential model for scaffolded, inquiry-based learning environments
Demonstrated how engineered classroom environments could simultaneously serve as research sites
key_works: Design Experiments: Theoretical and Methodological Challenges in Creating Complex Interventions in Classroom Settings (JLS, 1992)
Guided Discovery in a Community of Learners (1994)
notes: DBR originator. Fostering Communities of Learners. Core T17.

name: Marlene Scardamalia
role: Pioneer of knowledge-building communities and CSCL
description: Pioneer of knowledge-building communities and computer-supported collaborative learning. Co-developed Knowledge Forum (formerly CSILE), the first networked collaborative knowledge-building environment. With Bereiter, theorized the distinction between 'knowledge telling' and 'knowledge transforming' in writing and learning. Knowledge-building pedagogy influenced the design of modern collaborative learning systems and MOOCs.
key_contributions: Co-developed Knowledge Forum (formerly CSILE), the first networked collaborative knowledge-building environment
With Bereiter, theorized the distinction between 'knowledge telling' and 'knowledge transforming' in writing and learning
Established socio-cognitive frameworks for collaborative platforms that emphasize collective knowledge construction
Knowledge-building pedagogy influenced the design of modern collaborative learning systems and MOOCs
key_works: Computer Support for Knowledge-Building Communities (with Bereiter, JLS, 1994)
Knowledge Building: Theory, Pedagogy, and Technology (2006)
notes: Knowledge Forum / CSILE. CSCL pioneer.

name: Chris Dede
role: Pioneer of immersive learning environments; hybrid education researcher
description: Pioneer of immersive learning environments and hybrid education. 'Immersive interfaces for engagement and learning' (Science, 2009) has thousands of citations. Co-edited Learning Engineering for Online Education (Routledge, 2019) — the field's first major book. Post-pandemic work framed hybrid learning not as emergency measure but as optimal future state. Directed multiple NSF and DoE grants applying LE principles to K-12 and higher education.
key_contributions: Established engineering parameters for immersive virtual environments (VR/AR) in STEM education
Immersive interfaces for engagement and learning' (Science, 2009) has thousands of citations
Co-edited Learning Engineering for Online Education (Routledge, 2019) — the field's first major book
Post-pandemic work framed hybrid learning not as emergency measure but as optimal future state
Directed multiple NSF and DoE grants applying LE principles to K-12 and higher education
key_works: Immersive interfaces for engagement and learning (Science, 2009)
Learning Engineering for Online Education (co-editor, Routledge, 2019)
Scaling Up Success: Lessons from Technology-Based Educational Improvement (2006)
notes: VR/AR and hybrid learning. LE Online Education book co-editor.

name: Neil Heffernan
role: Creator of ASSISTments; large-scale LE experimentation pioneer
description: Director of Learning Sciences and Technologies at WPI and creator of ASSISTments, a free ITS serving 500,000+ students and 20,000+ teachers. Built E-TRIALS within ASSISTments — a platform for running randomized controlled experiments at scale in real classrooms. Pioneered 'crowdsourcing' of instructional design through teacher-authored hint sequences.
key_contributions: Created ASSISTments, a free ITS serving 500,000+ students and 20,000+ teachers
Built E-TRIALS within ASSISTments — a platform for running randomized controlled experiments at scale in real classrooms
Pioneered the 'crowdsourcing' of instructional design through teacher-authored hint sequences in ASSISTments
Demonstrated that formative assessment integrated with intelligent hints improves learning outcomes significantly
Research spans educational data mining, learning analytics, and optimization of student learning pathways
key_works: ASSISTments: Helping Mentor Students While Providing Teachers Feedback (ITS, 2004)
The Doer Effect: Replicating Findings that Doing Causes Learning (EDM, 2018)
notes: ASSISTments platform. Large-scale RCT experimentation in classrooms.

name: Karen Willcox
affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, Oden Institute
role: MIT OEPI lead; LE in higher education advocate; computational science-to-education bridge
description: Led MIT's Online Education Policy Initiative (OEPI), producing the 2016 catalyst report that re-introduced 'learning engineer' to higher education discourse. Argued that MOOCs and digital scale require a new professional class with learning science and engineering expertise. Connected learning engineering to computational science methodologies from aerospace engineering. Now at UT Austin's Oden Institute for Computational Engineering.
key_contributions: Led MIT's Online Education Policy Initiative (OEPI), producing the 2016 catalyst report that re-introduced 'learning engineer' to higher education discourse
Argued that MOOCs and digital scale require a new professional class with learning science + engineering expertise
Connected learning engineering to computational science methodologies from aerospace engineering
Helped establish MIT's residential + online hybrid model as a learning engineering showcase
key_works: Online Education: A Catalyst for Higher Education Reforms (with Sarma & Lippel, MIT OEPI, 2016)
notes: MIT OEPI lead. Re-introduced 'learning engineer' term to higher ed.

name: Albert T. Corbett
role: Co-developer of Bayesian Knowledge Tracing; ITS architecture pioneer
description: Co-developer of Bayesian Knowledge Tracing (BKT), the dominant probabilistic student model in ITS. Co-designed the ACT Programming Tutor and Algebra Cognitive Tutor with Anderson and Koedinger. Known for systematic empirical work on learning curves, error analysis, and mastery criteria in cognitive tutors.
key_contributions: Co-developed Bayesian Knowledge Tracing (BKT), the dominant probabilistic student model in ITS
Co-designed the ACT Programming Tutor and Algebra Cognitive Tutor with Anderson and Koedinger
Systematic empirical work on learning curves, error analysis, and mastery criteria in cognitive tutors
Established methods for validating ITS student models against controlled study outcomes
key_works: Intelligent tutoring systems (with Koedinger & Anderson, HCI Handbook, 1997)
Knowledge Tracing: Modeling the Acquisition of Procedural Knowledge (User Modeling and User-Adapted Interaction, 1994)
notes: BKT originator. Core ITS student modelling figure.

name: Robert R. Hoffman
affiliation: Institute for Human and Machine Cognition (IHMC), Pensacola FL
role: Cognitive task analysis pioneer; expertise and proficiency researcher; knowledge elicitation methodologist
description: Pioneer of cognitive task analysis and knowledge elicitation methodology at IHMC. Co-authored the field-defining practitioner guide Working Minds (MIT Press, 2006) with Gary Klein and Beth Crandall. Developed proficiency scale frameworks extending the Dreyfus model for training design. Active IEEE ICICLE contributor who brought human factors expertise research tradition to the LE field.
key_contributions: Developed the Critical Decision Method (CDM) for eliciting expert knowledge in naturalistic settings
Co-authored Working Minds — the field-defining practitioner guide to cognitive task analysis for training design
Applied proficiency scale frameworks (extended Dreyfus model) to learning engineering and training system design
With Klein, established naturalistic decision-making as a substrate for evidence-based training design
Active ICICLE contributor who brought human factors and expertise research traditions to the LE community
key_works: Crandall, Klein & Hoffman — Working Minds: A Practitioner's Guide to Cognitive Task Analysis (MIT Press, 2006)
Hoffman, Ward et al. — Accelerated Learning: Training for High Proficiency in a Complex World (APA, 2014)
Hoffman & Militello — Perspectives on Cognitive Task Analysis (CRC Press, 2008)
notes: IHMC. CTA and expertise research. ICICLE community figure.

name: Gary Klein
role: Naturalistic decision-making pioneer; Recognition-Primed Decision model creator
description: Creator of the Recognition-Primed Decision (RPD) model and Critical Decision Method. Founder of the naturalistic decision-making (NDM) field, which informs how experts are trained in high-stakes environments. Author of Sources of Power (MIT Press, 1998), widely read by LE practitioners for insight into expertise-based training design. Connects expertise science directly to instructional design for high-stakes professional contexts.
key_contributions: Created the Recognition-Primed Decision (RPD) model — the most influential descriptive model of expert decision-making under uncertainty
Co-founded the naturalistic decision-making (NDM) field, which informs training design in military, medical, and safety-critical contexts
Developed the Critical Decision Method for knowledge elicitation and training system design
Author of Sources of Power — widely read by LE practitioners as a foundational text on expertise-based training
Connects cognitive science of expertise directly to instructional design for high-stakes professional contexts
key_works: Sources of Power: How People Make Decisions (MIT Press, 1998)
Seeing What Others Don't: The Remarkable Ways We Gain Insights (PublicAffairs, 2013)
Working Minds: A Practitioner's Guide to Cognitive Task Analysis (with Crandall & Hoffman, MIT Press, 2006)
notes: NDM and RPD. Expertise-based training. Sources of Power.

name: Vincent Aleven
role: ITS researcher; pioneer of example-tracing tutors and help-seeking scaffolding
description: CMU HCII researcher who developed Example-Tracing Tutors — a new ITS paradigm requiring no cognitive model, built by demonstrating solution paths. Led research on the assistance dilemma (when to give vs. withhold help) and metacognitive scaffolding in ITS. Co-developed deployed math tutors including Lynnette.
key_contributions: Developed Example-Tracing Tutors — a new ITS paradigm requiring no cognitive model, built by demonstrating solution paths

key_contributions.Led research on the assistance dilemma: when to give vs. withhold help to maximize learning

key_contributions: Demonstrated metacognitive scaffolding in ITS can improve help-seeking behavior, not just domain knowledge
Co-developed Lynnette and other deployed math tutors at CMU HCII
Extended Cognitive Tutor methodology to collaborative and peer-learning contexts
key_works: An effective metacognitive strategy: learning by doing and explaining with a Cognitive Tutor (Cognitive Science, 2002)
Exploring the assistance dilemma in experiments with Cognitive Tutors (Educ Psychology Review, 2007)
A new paradigm for intelligent tutoring systems: Example-Tracing Tutors (IJAIED, 2009)
notes: Example-Tracing Tutors. Assistance dilemma research.

name: Carolyn Rosé
affiliation: Carnegie Mellon University, HCII / Language Technologies Institute
role: NLP researcher; pioneer of computational analysis of collaborative learning discourse
description: CMU researcher who applied NLP to analyze and scaffold collaborative learning conversations in real time. Developed the BASILICA architecture for conversational agents and the Bazaar collaborative learning platform. Bridged computational linguistics and CSCL, founding a distinct NLP-for-learning subfield.
key_contributions: Applied NLP to analyze and scaffold collaborative learning conversations in real time
Developed BASILICA architecture for conversational agents that support collaborative learning
Showed that automated discourse analysis can detect productive vs. unproductive collaboration patterns
Led development of the Bazaar collaborative learning platform within the OpenSimon toolkit
Bridged computational linguistics and CSCL, founding a distinct NLP-for-learning subfield
key_works: Analyzing collaborative learning processes automatically (IJAIED, 2008)
Supportive technologies for collaborative learning in ill-structured domains (CSCL, 2008)
Linguistic correlates of conversational uptake in collaborative learning discussions (IJAIED, 2013)
notes: NLP for collaborative learning. BASILICA and Bazaar.

name: Jack Mostow
role: Pioneer of AI-powered reading tutors; data-driven ITS evaluation methods
description: Created Project LISTEN — the first ITS that listens to children read aloud and provides real-time corrective feedback using automated speech recognition. Developed data-driven methods for evaluating ITS effectiveness using log data from deployed systems.
key_contributions: Created Project LISTEN — the first ITS that listens to children read aloud and provides real-time corrective feedback
Demonstrated that automated speech recognition can detect oral reading errors with sufficient accuracy for tutoring
Developed data-driven methods for evaluating ITS effectiveness using log data from deployed systems
Contributed tactics for mining, mapping, and modeling data from intelligent tutors
Applied learning engineering principles to literacy acquisition in elementary school settings
key_works: Listening to children read aloud (Project LISTEN, 1999)
Some useful tactics to modify, map and mine data from intelligent tutors (Natural Language Engineering, 2006)
Data-driven approaches to evaluating and improving reading tutors (AIED, 2007)
notes: Project LISTEN. Reading tutors. Data-driven ITS evaluation.

name: Rod Roscoe
affiliation: Arizona State University, Learning Engineering Institute
role: Writing ITS researcher; human factors in automated writing feedback
description: ASU Learning Engineering Institute researcher who co-led the Writing Pal ITS — a game-based writing tutor with automated essay scoring and strategy training. Investigated usability and metacognitive dimensions of writing intelligent tutoring systems and how learners interact with and trust automated feedback.
key_contributions: Co-led the Writing Pal ITS — a game-based writing tutor with automated essay scoring and strategy training
Investigated usability and metacognitive dimensions of writing intelligent tutoring systems
Demonstrated that automated feedback can improve student revision behavior if appropriately scaffolded
Studied how learners interact with and trust automated writing feedback systems
Connected human-computer interaction principles to writing instruction at scale
key_works: Writing Pal intelligent tutoring system: usability testing and observations (Computers in Human Behavior, 2014)
Assessing and supporting learners' metacognitive awareness of writing (Metacognition and Learning, 2011)
Automated feedback and student revisions (JEDM, 2017)
notes: Writing Pal ITS. ASU Learning Engineering Institute.

name: Robert K. Atkinson
role: Instructional design researcher; worked examples and pedagogical agents
description: ASU researcher who demonstrated that optimized sequencing of worked examples using animated pedagogical agents improves learning. Applied cognitive load theory principles to the design of example-based instruction and investigated the guidance fading effect across different instructional formats.
key_contributions: Demonstrated that optimized sequencing of worked examples using animated pedagogical agents improves learning
Synthesized evidence on transitioning learners from example study to independent problem solving
Showed that interactive worked examples encourage more effective processing than static presentations
Applied cognitive load theory principles to the design of example-based instruction
Investigated how the guidance fading effect operates across different instructional formats
key_works: Optimizing learning from examples using animated pedagogical agents (JEP Applied, 2002)
Transitioning from studying examples to solving problems (JEP Applied, 2003)
Interactive example-based learning environments (Educ Psychology Review, 2007)
notes: Worked examples and pedagogical agents. CLT applications.

name: Mina C. Johnson-Glenberg
affiliation: Arizona State University, Embodied Games Lab
role: XR and embodied learning researcher; mixed reality STEM education
description: Directs the Embodied Games Lab at ASU. Demonstrated that embodied interaction in mixed reality environments improves STEM concept learning. Developed design guidelines for immersive learning environments based on embodied cognition theory. Positioned XR as a rigorous LE domain with measurable outcome metrics.
key_contributions: Demonstrated that embodied interaction in mixed reality environments improves STEM concept learning
Developed the Embodied Games Lab at ASU focused on XR for science and engineering education
Showed that whole-body interaction encodes physics concepts more durably than screen-based instruction
Contributed design guidelines for immersive learning environments based on embodied cognition theory
Positioned XR as a rigorous learning engineering domain with measurable outcome metrics
key_works: Embodied science and mixed reality: how gesture and motion capture affect physics education (JLS, 2014)
Grand challenges in immersive learning environments and XR (Frontiers in Education, 2018)
Powering up and slowing down: Technology and embodied STEM learning (J of Science Ed and Technology, 2016)
notes: Embodied Games Lab. XR for STEM. Embodied cognition in learning.

name: Arthur C. Graesser
affiliation: University of Memphis, Institute for Intelligent Systems
role: AutoTutor developer; discourse analysis and comprehension in ITS
description: Created AutoTutor — an ITS that conducts Socratic dialogue in natural language, covering physics, computer literacy, and other domains. Showed AutoTutor produces deep learning gains comparable to expert human tutors. Co-developed Coh-Metrix for analyzing textual cohesion and readability. Co-founded the discourse-based approach to ITS.
key_contributions: Created AutoTutor — an ITS that conducts socratic dialogue in natural language, covering physics, computer literacy, and other domains
Showed that AutoTutor produces deep learning gains comparable to expert human tutors in one-on-one sessions
Developed Coh-Metrix — a computational tool for analyzing the cohesion and readability of educational texts
Demonstrated that conversational tutoring dialogues support deeper conceptual understanding than worked-example ITS
Co-founded the discourse-based approach to ITS that influenced conversational AI tutors
key_works: AutoTutor: A tutor with dialogue in natural language (IEEE TLT, 2005)
Intelligent tutoring systems: past, present, and future (Handbook of Research on Educ Comms and Tech, 2004)
Collaborative tutoring discourse (Cognition and Instruction, 1995)
notes: AutoTutor and Coh-Metrix. Natural language dialogue in ITS.

name: Philip I. Pavlik Jr.
affiliation: University of Memphis, Institute for Intelligent Systems
role: Cognitive modeling researcher; spacing and retrieval practice optimization in ITS
description: Developed Performance Factors Analysis (PFA) — a student model accounting for both practice and forgetting. Derived an activation-based model of the spacing effect and created algorithms for optimal practice scheduling. Contributed to Learning Factors Analysis (LFA) for data-driven discovery of knowledge components.
key_contributions: Developed Performance Factors Analysis (PFA) — a student model accounting for both practice and forgetting
Derived an activation-based model of the spacing effect and its application to vocabulary learning
Created algorithms for computing optimal practice schedules based on forgetting curve parameters
Contributed to Learning Factors Analysis (LFA) for data-driven discovery of knowledge components
Applied cognitive models of memory decay to personalized practice scheduling in ITS platforms
key_works: Performance factors analysis: A new alternative to knowledge tracing (AIED, 2009)
Practice and forgetting effects on vocabulary memory (Cognitive Science, 2005)
Using a model to compute the optimal schedule of practice (JEP Applied, 2007)
notes: PFA student model. Spacing and forgetting curves. LFA.

name: Ashok K. Goel
affiliation: Georgia Institute of Technology, School of Interactive Computing
role: AI educator; creator of Jill Watson AI teaching assistant
description: Created Jill Watson — one of the first AI teaching assistants deployed at scale in a university course, handling 40,000+ student questions with near-human accuracy. Applied case-based reasoning to educational question-answering. Led the AI4K12 initiative developing national guidelines for K-12 AI literacy education.
key_contributions: Created Jill Watson — one of the first AI teaching assistants deployed at scale in a university course, handling 40,000+ student questions
Demonstrated that AI TAs can operate at near-human accuracy without students detecting they are AI
Applied case-based reasoning and knowledge graphs to educational question-answering at scale
Led AI4K12 initiative developing national guidelines for K–12 AI literacy education
Showed that AI-augmented instruction frees human TAs for higher-order mentoring
key_works: Jill Watson: A virtual teaching assistant for online education (ICLS, 2016)
Thinking critically about virtual teaching assistance for online education (AI Matters, 2016)
AI4K12: Developing national guidelines for K-12 artificial intelligence education (AAAI, 2023)
notes: Jill Watson AI TA. AI4K12. Case-based reasoning in education.

name: Sidney K. D'Mello
affiliation: University of Colorado Boulder, Institute of Cognitive Science
role: Affective computing researcher; emotion detection in intelligent tutoring
description: Demonstrated that confusion, when productively resolved, benefits deep learning in ITS contexts. Developed multimodal sensors (eye tracking, posture, facial expression) for real-time affect detection during learning. Mapped the dynamics of affective states (boredom, confusion, frustration, flow) during complex learning tasks.
key_contributions: Demonstrated that confusion, when productively resolved, is beneficial for deep learning in ITS contexts
Developed multimodal sensors (eye tracking, posture, facial expression) for real-time affect detection during learning
Showed that AutoTutor's affective-aware branching improves learning outcomes over non-affective versions
Mapped the dynamics of affective states (boredom, confusion, frustration, flow) during complex learning tasks
Applied gaze-based modeling to study reading comprehension in literary text contexts
key_works: Confusion is beneficial for learning (Learning and Instruction, 2014)
Dynamics of affective states during complex learning (Learning and Instruction, 2012)
AutoTutor improves deep learning of computer literacy (AIED, 2007)
notes: Affective computing in ITS. Confusion and learning. Multimodal sensing.

name: Justin Reich
role: EdTech researcher; critic of MOOC hype; evidence-based online learning advocate
description: Authored Failure to Disrupt (MIT Press, 2020) — the most rigorous critique of MOOC platforms' failure to improve educational equity despite scale. Demonstrated empirically that MOOCs disproportionately benefit already-advantaged learners. Leads MIT Teaching Systems Lab, studying how teacher learning and practice change with educational technology.
key_contributions: Authored Failure to Disrupt — the most rigorous critique of MOOC platforms' failure to improve educational equity despite scale
Demonstrated empirically that MOOCs disproportionately benefit already-advantaged learners, contradicting disruption narratives

key_contributions.Documented the MOOC pivot: how platforms shifted from open access toward credentialed professional learning

key_contributions: Advocated for evidence-based online learning design grounded in learning sciences rather than technology novelty
Leads MIT Teaching Systems Lab, studying how teacher learning and practice change with educational technology
key_works: Failure to Disrupt: Why Technology Alone Can't Transform Education (MIT Press, 2020)
Rebooting MOOC research (Science, 2015)
The MOOC pivot (Science, 2019)
notes: Failure to Disrupt. MOOC evidence. MIT Teaching Systems Lab.

name: Bruce M. McLaren
role: ITS researcher; learning games and worked-example sequencing in mathematics
description: CMU HCII research scientist who designed Decimal Point — a math game for decimal numbers shown to outperform conventional tutoring software in controlled trials. Investigated optimal sequencing of worked examples and tutored problem solving, and developed adaptive worked-example and ITS hybrid approaches for fraction and decimal learning.
key_contributions: Designed Decimal Point — a math game for decimal numbers shown to produce learning gains over conventional tutoring software
Showed that computer games can promote mathematics learning more effectively than conventional ITS in controlled trials
Investigated optimal sequencing of worked examples and tutored problem solving, challenging assumptions about when each helps
Developed adaptive worked-example and ITS hybrid approaches for fraction and decimal learning
Contributed foundational research on example-tracing tutors at CMU HCII alongside Vincent Aleven
key_works: A computer game promotes mathematics learning more than a conventional approach (IJAIED, 2017)
Decimal Point: Designing and evaluating a math game for decimal numbers (ITS, 2014)
Worked examples and intelligent tutoring in fraction learning (ITS, 2016)
notes: Decimal Point math game. Worked example sequencing. CMU HCII.

name: Ken Holstein
role: Human-AI complementarity researcher; equity in adaptive learning; real-time teacher orchestration
description: CMU HCII researcher who developed real-time multimodal classroom systems giving teachers live student-struggle signals during ITS sessions. Demonstrated that teacher-AI complementarity — not replacement — yields better outcomes. Published the most-cited work on equity and fairness in AI-driven educational systems.
key_contributions: Developed real-time multimodal classroom systems giving teachers live student-struggle signals during ITS sessions
Demonstrated that teacher-AI complementarity — not replacement — yields better outcomes than either alone
Co-designed orchestration tools with teachers through participatory design, making teacher agency central to LE systems
Published the most-cited work on equity and fairness in AI-driven educational systems
Leads the new wave of learning engineering that foregrounds human oversight, equity, and participatory design
key_works: Improving at-scale ITS: how multimodal AI enables real-time precision (CHI, 2018)
Co-designing a real-time classroom orchestration tool to support teacher-AI complementarity (CHI, 2019)
Equity and AI in education: will AIEd amplify or alleviate inequities? (chapter, 2021)
notes: Teacher-AI complementarity. Equity in AIEd. Real-time orchestration.

name: Erin Walker
affiliation: Arizona State University (formerly Carnegie Mellon University, HCII)
role: ITS and peer-tutoring researcher; collaborative learning scaffolding
description: Developed ITS scaffolding systems that support peer tutoring in computer science, bridging collaborative and adaptive learning. Showed intelligent support for peer tutoring outperforms unscaffolded peer tutoring and approaches one-on-one ITS. Bridges CMU HCII cognitive tutor lineage with ASU's collaborative learning traditions.
key_contributions: Developed ITS scaffolding systems that support peer tutoring in computer science, bridging collaborative and adaptive learning
Showed that intelligent support for peer tutoring outperforms unscaffolded peer tutoring and approaches one-on-one ITS
Investigated how students learn from overhearing dialogues in virtual tutoring sessions
Applied conversational scaffolding to CSCL contexts using dialogue-based ITS agents
Bridges CMU HCII cognitive tutor lineage with ASU's human systems and collaborative learning traditions
key_works: Adaptive intelligent support to improve peer tutoring in CS (AIED, 2011)
Designing for problem solving: ITS scaffolding for peer tutoring (CSCL, 2014)
Overhearing dialogues and monologues in virtual tutoring sessions (Cognitive Science, 2009)
notes: Peer tutoring ITS. CMU-to-ASU bridge. Collaborative learning scaffolding.

name: Danielle S. McNamara
affiliation: University of Southern California (formerly Arizona State University)
role: Reading and writing ITS researcher; Coh-Metrix and iSTART developer
description: Created iSTART (Interactive Strategy Training for Active Reading and Thinking) and co-developed Coh-Metrix — the most widely used computational tool for analyzing textual cohesion, readability, and discourse structure. Co-led Writing Pal — the first ITS for writing strategy instruction. Most-cited ASU learning engineering researcher; moved to USC circa 2018.
key_contributions: Created iSTART (Interactive Strategy Training for Active Reading and Thinking) — an ITS for reading comprehension strategy instruction
Co-developed Coh-Metrix — the most widely used computational tool for analyzing textual cohesion, readability, and discourse structure
Co-led Writing Pal — the first ITS for writing strategy instruction with automated essay scoring feedback
Developed a comprehensive model of reading comprehension integrating memory, inference, and strategy components
Most-cited ASU learning engineering researcher; moved to USC ~2018
key_works: iSTART: Interactive strategy training for active reading and thinking (JEP Applied, 2004)
Coh-Metrix: Analysis of text on cohesion and language (Behavior Research Methods, 2004)
Automated Evaluation of Text and Discourse with Coh-Metrix (Cambridge UP, 2014)
notes: iSTART and Coh-Metrix. Writing Pal ITS. Text analytics for education.

name: Janet L. Kolodner
role: Case-based reasoning pioneer; Learning by Design framework developer; co-author of LE Toolkit
description: Created the case-based reasoning (CBR) paradigm and developed the Learning by Design (LBD) framework — project-based learning grounded in design challenges. Co-edited the Learning Engineering Toolkit with Jim Goodell. Intellectual parent of the LE process methodology: iterative, evidence-based, user-centered design of learning systems.
key_contributions: Created the case-based reasoning (CBR) paradigm — a model of learning from experience that informs how ITS should structure analogical transfer
Developed the Learning by Design (LBD) framework — project-based learning grounded in design challenges
Co-edited the Learning Engineering Toolkit with Jim Goodell, the field's primary practitioner handbook
Showed that problem-based learning combined with case-based reasoning produces deeper science understanding in middle school

key_contributions.Intellectual parent of the LE process me: iterative, evidence-based, user-centered design of learning systems

key_works: Case-Based Reasoning (Morgan Kaufmann, 1993)
Problem-based learning meets case-based reasoning in the middle-school science classroom (JLS, 2003)
Learning by Design: Creating opportunities for learning in after-school programs (Instructional Science, 2003)
notes: Case-based reasoning. Learning by Design. LE Toolkit co-editor.

name: Daniel L. Schwartz
role: Learning scientist; inventing-before-learning and productive failure paradigm developer
description: Developed the 'time for telling' principle and 'inventing to prepare for future learning' — showing that generative struggle creates representations that support deeper subsequent learning. Established the productive failure literature demonstrating that effortful incorrect attempts precede better understanding than direct instruction.

key_contributions.Developed the 'time for telling' princip: learners benefit more from instruction after attempting to generate solutions themselves

key_contributions: Coined 'inventing to prepare for future learning' — showing that generative struggle creates representations that support deeper subsequent learning
Showed that practice with contrasting cases produces better transfer than problem-solving practice alone
Established the productive failure literature demonstrating that effortful incorrect attempts precede better understanding than direct instruction
Co-authored The ABCs of How We Learn — a synthesis of 26 learning mechanisms bridging science and instructional design
key_works: A time for telling (Cognition and Instruction, 1998)
Inventing to prepare for future learning (Cognition and Instruction, 2004)
The ABCs of How We Learn (Norton, 2016)
notes: Time for telling / productive failure. Inventing-before-learning.

name: Paul A. Kirschner
role: CLT applied to collaboration; co-author of 'Why Minimal Guidance Fails
description: Co-authored 'Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work' (2006) — the most-cited critique of discovery and problem-based learning (6,800+ citations). Extended Cognitive Load Theory to collaborative learning. Co-authored How Learning Happens with Carl Hendrick and the 4C/ID model with van Merriënboer.
key_contributions: Co-authored 'Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work' — the most-cited critique of discovery and problem-based learning (6,800+ citations)
Extended Cognitive Load Theory to collaborative learning, showing that collaboration can increase or decrease load depending on design
Co-authored How Learning Happens with Carl Hendrick — a practitioner guide to 28 seminal educational psychology works
Challenged widespread ed-tech myths (learning styles, digital natives, multitasking) with rigorous empirical evidence
Co-authored the 4C/ID model with van Merriënboer, a widely used framework for complex-skills instructional design
key_works: Why Minimal Guidance During Instruction Does Not Work (with Sweller & Clark, Educ Psychologist, 2006)
A cognitive load approach to collaborative learning (Educ Psychology Review, 2009)
How Learning Happens: Seminal Works in Educational Psychology (with Hendrick, Routledge, 2020)
notes: Why Minimal Guidance Fails'. CLT for collaboration. How Learning Happens.

name: Dragan Gašević
role: Learning analytics field-builder; LAK conference co-founder; process mining in CSCL
description: Co-founded the Learning Analytics and Knowledge (LAK) conference and the Society for Learning Analytics Research (SoLAR). Established the principle that 'learning analytics should not promote one-size-fits-all' — foundational for equity-aware LE system design. Applied process mining to collaborative learning data, revealing strategy patterns invisible to aggregate statistics.
key_contributions: Co-founded the Learning Analytics and Knowledge (LAK) conference and the Society for Learning Analytics Research (SoLAR)
Established the principle that 'learning analytics should not promote one-size-fits-all' — foundational for equity-aware LE system design
Applied process mining techniques to collaborative learning data, revealing learning strategy patterns invisible to aggregate statistics
Demonstrated that analytics without theory produce misleading conclusions about learning
Critical voice ensuring LE analytics systems remain grounded in learning science, not just data science
key_works: Let's not forget: learning analytics are about learning (TechTrends, 2015)
Learning analytics should not promote one size fits all (Internet and Higher Education, 2017)
Effects of the use of analytics in individualized student support (Computers & Education, 2016)
notes: LAK and SoLAR co-founder. Analytics must serve learning theory.
